paragraphs.body: Modeling an Unconventional Essay: Son of Mr. Green Jeans, an Essay on Fatherhood by Dinty Moore.

August 2011, we met for the first time on the bus to our first day of kindergarten. Hot air whooshes through the windows and the sound of wheels crushing into pavement makes it hard for us to speak. On this day, I learned how to introduce myself to someone.
Bob haircut. From Kindergarten to second grade, you had a little brown bob. Your hair was so thick that you couldn’t tuck all of it behind your ears.

Can we make up a dance? After scouring the King of Prussia mall, we couldn’t wait to come home and start choreographing. Every time “Night Changes” by One Direction shuffles, I can smell your carpet and hear the way you tried to catch your breath as we laughed so hard.

Daddy daughter dance 2013. I wore a pearl necklace that was way too big, and your dress was bright blue. We held hands as our plastic shoes squeaked on the gym floor while “Cotton eyed Joe” blared. Your hair spun as you jumped, and I realized how fun dancing can be.

Every summer in middle school, we walked the train tracks behind my neighborhood. Our backpacks contained bathing suits, a towel for each of us, and your brother’s old speaker. We talked about everyone we knew and when we made it to the bridge, you never made fun of me for being afraid of heights.

Fridays. We begged our parents to write bus notes allowing us to ride to each other's houses. Occasionally, the driver would turn on Q102 and we would all sing along to whichever Micheal Jackson song came on. Your smile had so many gaps from missing baby teeth, and I laughed watching you try to hit the song's high notes.

Griffey! Your parents swore they wouldn’t get you and your siblings a dog and they finally caved in the beginning of third grade. Griffey is 10 now and doesn’t chase us around the backyard anymore. But he still greets me at the door with his high-pitched whistles and wagging tail.

“Hello" by Adele came out in 2015, I made you listen to it while we sat on my bed watching the butterfly stickers on my fan spin. Today, “Hello” transports me back to my purple walls and light green bed sheets.

I Sometimes think about how long we have been a part of each other's lives and do not understand how we never drifted. Our interests and schedules are so different it’s almost laughable, and yet we still make an effort with each other.

Jake! In our freshman year of high school, your parents brought home your second dog. We made every excuse to play with him as much as possible and I admit, sometimes I asked you to hangout just to see him.

annotations.text: I admit, sometimes I asked you to hangout just to see him.
annotations.comment: Ha! I often wondered if my friends were coming to see me or my dog when I was young.

paragraphs.body: “Kids” by MGMT. On our walks along the train tracks, we must have played Kids hundreds of times. We got to a point where we could sing every lyric without messing it up. Both of our voices were horrible, and I can still feel the way my legs ached as we continued to walk.

Laughing. There is no one else I have laughed harder with. It is the thing I miss most about you, the way your mouth opens and your eyes close. When you really laugh there are no noises or squawks, you are just trying to breathe.

Morgan Wallen. When we went to South Carolina in the summer of Junior year, you tried your best to make me like country music. Specifically, Morgan Wallen. You played me all of your favorite songs and every time they finished you asked me, “Still no?” and every time I nodded my head. Somehow, this didn’t frustrate you and you have just accepted that country isn’t my thing. You taught me that maybe I should be more accepting too.

annotations.text: Somehow, this didn’t frustrate you and you have just accepted that country isn’t my thing. You taught me that maybe I should be more accepting
annotations.comment: This is a really cool life lesson through your interactions :)

paragraphs.body: Your first car was a blue 2013 Nissan. A stale smell from your brother's old baseball equipment would hit my nose every time we got in. We drove all over the county listening to Kanye West's Graduation and were so excited to put the windows down when it hit April. Watching you drive, I learned that sometimes going over the speed limit isn’t always necessary and how to navigate a four way stop sign.

October in high school. We were always so excited because the fall menu at Starbucks would be back. The air would be crisp and your dad’s jeep would have its doors back on. We would leave school usually missing math or a study hall and turn on SOS by Sza. You wouldn’t have to wear sunglasses while driving anymore because it was beginning to get darker.

Planning. The past fourteen years have been of careful planning. You are such a busy person yet always find time for me. Whenever I mention a concert we should go to or a day I want to come visit you, immediately you check your calendar and ask me, “What day again?” Planning isn’t always easy and finding time is inconvenient, yet you always do it without complaining. I aim to make time for the people I care about the way you do.
Queen. “Somebody to Love” is your favorite and every time it plays, I think of you.

annotations.text: is your favorite and every time it plays, I think of you.
annotations.comment: This is really cool, but I wonder if you could recall a specific time you've heard the song recently and describe that. Where were you? What was the song playing on? What were you thinking about, specifically?

paragraphs.body: Real. Meeting new people is not easy because you do not actually know what they are like. But I have always thought that you show people exactly who you are the moment you meet them. You smile and introduce yourself so naturally and this kindness is still something you show every day.

Softball. There has not been a time when softball hasn’t consumed your life. Watching you move up travel teams and eventually into a division one collegiate program is so inspiring and I’m so proud of you! It is so inspiring to watch you put so much effort into something you love. Especially when the effort pays off.

Traditions. Whenever a new season of Stranger Things comes out, it is usually summer. We sit in the corner of your couch and make Shirley temples. Sometimes the episodes are so long that we fall asleep and must rewind the parts we missed. It will be 4 years that we’ve done this when the final season premiers.

“Under Pressure” by Queen was one of my first favorite songs. When I showed it to you we were in my kitchen and had just come home from elementary school. We danced and got snacks from my old black and white fridge and I’m sure we couldn’t understand any of the lyrics.

Victorious. Our favorite show growing up. The amount of times we sat in each other's living rooms laughing at the jokes and singing along to the different songs is uncountable. We are too old for it now but still watch anyway and the song “My Best Friend’s Brother” will always remind me of your old green couch in your living room.

Will I ever be able to repay you for all of the good you have done for me? I don’t think so, but I can thank you. Thank you for teaching me how to be a friend and how to treat other people, for showing me how to be mature, and for so many little things I don’t think you even realize you showed me.

X-rated movies and horror have never been your thing. The one time we tried to watch Ari Aster’s Hereditary will probably be the last time we choose a scary movie and I'm sorry for being the one to suggest it!

Your parents have never failed to make me feel welcome. They always greet me with a hug and have gotten me presents on Christmas. I feel so lucky to be so included in everything anytime I come over and I hope that my family makes you feel the same way.

Zero. There are zero days that we haven't texted since moving away from each other. There are zero people I would call before you. Zero weeks that I don’t wonder how you’re doing. It doesn’t seem possible to form a friendship that resembles ours with someone else and I’m so grateful to you, Kelly.

annotations.comment: Hello, This essay makes your relationship very clear from the opening sentence to the final line. The central focusing idea of this piece is clearly that your friendship with Kelly is one of the most important experiences of your life, and this is supported by your big ideas centering on your celebration of so many of those specific moments and overall feelings. The words that stand out the most to me are singing, dancing, and laughing and all of their variations. It’s clear that these activities are integral to your friendship, and they have existed throughout your time together. You do a great job of integrating specific songs from different moments in your life and describing how those songs created the moments that helped to form and reinforce your bond. What I admire most about your essay is the celebration I mentioned above. You focus intensely throughout this piece on the best parts of your friend and the best parts of the time you’ve spent with her, what she means to you, what you’ve learned from her. There is not a passage in this essay that even hints at any negativity or sadness. Obviously, the primary audience of this essay is your friend Kelly, as you use the second-person narration throughout the piece and mention her by name. But I also see a way in which this is an essay written for so many other young people going through the separation anxiety of being apart from a loved one for the first time. The essay is a celebration, not only of your time with your friend, but also of friendship in general, the importance of those bonds. What I learned from reading your essay is that you are a very loyal friend, that you value having fun and enjoying life, that you have dedicated a lot of your mind and your time to this, I’ll say it again, celebration of your friend and your friendship. It’s really admirable that you can take this relationship and turn it into an essay like this. It was a pleasure reading your essay and getting to know you better through your own experiences and observations.
